P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322.5662 
Edward T. Clifford 
Ter ry Appl ing 
Administration

Greve, Clifford, DiepenbrQck 
and Paras 
1000 G Street, Suite 400 . 
Sacramento~ CA 
95814-a885 
Executive/legol 
322·5901 
May 10, 1983 
Enforcement 
322·6441 
Statements of Economic I nteres! 
322-6444 
Re: 
Our Advice No. A-83-103 
Dear Mr. Clifford and Ms. Appling: 
Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of the 
Paradise Irrigation District (hereinafter "District"). 
The 
facts, as presented to me, are that the Distrlct is going to 
improve its water filtration system. 
General plans were sub-
mitted by, among otners, Frank Clendenen, the president and 
principal shareholder of Clendenen Engineers. 
The system he 
proposed is a "Modular Filtration System," which is manufactured 
by Sierra Tech Systems, Inc. 
Mr. Clendenen is the president and 
100% shareholder of tnis company. 
The District approved 
Mr. Clendenen's proposal and he has been nired to act as tne 
general engineer for the construction of the system. 
Your 
question is whetner Mr. Clendenen will nave a conflict of 
interest in completing the contract because of his financial 
interest in Sierra Tech Systems, Inc. 
DISCUSSION 
Government Code Section 871001/ prohibits a local govern-
ment official from making, or participating in the making of, a 
governmental decision in whicn he knows or has reason to know he 
has a financial interest. 
An official has a financial interest 
in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is 
reasonably foreseeable that tne decision will have a material 
financial effect on: 
!/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 


Edward T. Clifford 
Terry Appling 
May 10, 1983 
Page 2 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment wortn more 
than $1,000. 
* * * 
(c) 
Any source of income ... aggregating $250 or 
more in value provided to 
the pUblic official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is 
made. 
( d) 
official 
Any business entity in which the public 
is ... [an] officer .... 
For purposes of the Political Reform Act, the term "public 
official" includes consultants of local government agencies. 
(2 
Ca 1. Adm. Code Sect~on 18700 (a) .) 
A "consul tant" is a natural 
person who provides, under contract, information, advice, 
recommendation or counsel to a local government agency. 
(2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Section 18700 (a) (2) . ) 
This term does not include a 
person who: 
(A) 
Conducts researCh and arrives at conclusions 
witn respect to his or her rendition of information, 
advice, recommendation or counsel independent of the 
control and direction of the agency or any agency 
official, other than normal contract monitoring; and 
(B) 
Possesses no authority with respect to any 
agency decision beyond the rendition of information, 
advice, recommendation or counsel. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18700(a) (2) (A) and (B). 
When an engineer is hired under a·contract to perform 
engineering services for a specific project, he ordinarily comes 
within this exemption. 
Under such circumstances, the engineer uses 
his own expertise to render professional services according to the 
specifications of a contract, and his judgment is not subject to 
day-to-day review or direct~on by the District. 
Essentially the 
engineer is being called upon to deliver a finished project and not 


Edward T. Clifford 
Terry Appling 
May 10,1983 
Page 3 
to participate in or advise the District on general governmental 
decisions requiring engineering expertise.l/ 
In the present case, under tne facts you have provlded, 
Mr. Clendenen is not a consultant under tne Act. 
He nas been 
hired to construct a "Modular Filtration System" and he will 
complete the project according to the specifications of the 
contract. 
If there are deviations from the contract, such as 
substitutions, tnese decisions will ultimately be decided by the 
District Board, not. Mr. Clendenen. 
Because Mr. Clendenen is not 
a consultant, ne is not subject to the conflict of interests 
provisions of the Act. 
However, in tne future, if tne District nires an engineer to 
work on other than a particular project, or provides an engineer 
with more authority than Mr. Clendenen possesses in the present 
situation, the District should contact us for additional advice 
as to whetner these engineers are consultants within the meaning 
of tne Act. 
I nope I have answered all of your questions. 
If I can be 
of any additional help, please feel free to contact me at 
916/322-5901. 
Very truly yours, 
J~ha~~:;J{:C 
etuv 
Counsel, Legal Division 
JSM: km 
~/ This would be true even if some incidental advice 
about the specific proJect were provided as part of these 
professional services. 
For example, an engineer can advise a 
District on what materials should be used for construction, or 
that tne proJect could not be constructed within tne proposed 
budget. 


CLAI RE H. GREVE 
EDWARD T. CLIFFORD 
ANTHONY C. DIEPENBROCK 
GEO RGE E. Pi\RAS 
GFRARD A. ROSE

WI LLiAM C. BARRY 
THOMAS S. KNOX 
GARY SCOTT DECKE R 
TERRY A. APPLING 
QUINN D. DIMAND 
DENNIS W. RICHARDSON 
LO U IS J. ANA POLSKY 
GARY L. VINSON 
DOUGLAS L. YOUMANS 
."-NDREW D. WOLL 
STEFl\N L. MANOL"-KAS 
BR.'\DLEY R. LARSON 
LAW OFFICES OF 
CREVE, CLIFFORD, DIEPENBROCK S PARAS 
'J3S0 C STREET, SU ITE 400 
ENio. CALIFORNIA 95814-0885 
(916) 443-2011 
April 7, 1983 
Ms. Barbara Milman, Esq. 
Fair Political Practices 
Commission 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 
95804 
OF COU"-iSEL 
IRVING H. PERLUSS 
GERALD R. JOH:-':SON (1897-1973) 
1750 MONTGOMERY STREET 
SAN FRA:-':CISCO. CALIFORNIA 94111 
(415) 433-9270 
REPLY TO SACRAMENTO 
TELECOPIER 
(916) 441-7457 
Re: 
Proposed Agreement between Paradise Irrigation 
District and Clenden:en EnS_ineers __________ _ 
Dear Ms. Milman: 
This office represents Clendenen Engineers with 
respect to a proposed contract for the design and construction 
supervision of a water treatment plant for the Paradise 
Irrigation District. 
In the course of its review of the pro-
posed agreement in this matter, counsel for the District raised 
the possibility that a potential for a conflict of interest 
might exist with respect to Mr. Clendenen's interest in the 
company manufacturing the filtration system which has been 
selected by the District for use in its water treatment facility. 
Counsel for the District later prepared an opinion to the effect 
that, in their opinion, no conflict of interest exists. 
A copy 
of their opinion is enclosed for your review. 
In the course of a telephone conversation with Ms. 
Janice McLean, of your office, we were informed that the FPPC 
requests the following information in assessing whether or not 
a conflict of interest exists within the meaning of the Fair 
Political Practices Act: 
1. 
The relationship of Clendenen Engineers 
to the Paradise Irrigation District (PID), and the ser-
vices they are to perform, to wit, whether or not Mr. 


CREVE. CLIFFORD. DIEPENBROCK S PARAS 
Barbara Milman, Esq. 
April 7, 1983 
Page two 
Clendenen is a consultant and thereby a public employee 
within the meaning of the Act; 
2. 
The interest of Clendenen Engineers in 
Sierra Tech Systems, the patent holder and manufacturer 
of the filtration system; 
3. 
The decision to be made by Clendenen 
Engineers pursuant to its agreement with the PIDi 
4. 
The role of the PID in the design of the 
water treatment system; 
5. 
The financial impact of the PID/Clendenen 
agreement upon Sierra Tech Systems, Inc.; and, 
6. 
The financial status of Sierra Tech 
Systems, Inc., and whether or not the implementation of 
the proposed system will confer any special benefit on 
said corporation. 
This information is outlined for you below. 
Mr. 
Frank Clendenen is the President and principal shareholder of 
Clendenen Engineers, a California corporation. 
Mr. Clendenen is 
also the president and 100% shareholder of Sierra Tech Systems, 
Inc., the patent holder and manufacturer of MODULAR FILTER SYSTEM. 
In the summer of 1982, the PID expressed an interest 
in improving its water filtration system. 
This intent was acted 
upon by Clendenen Engineers as well as several engineering firms, 
who submitted general plans for improvements of the PID's filtra-
tion system. 
On September 1, 1982, the Board of the Irrigation 
District invited Clendenen Engineers, CH2M Hill of Redding, and 
GDA Engineering Survey and Planning to submit filtration plan 
concepts to the Board at its next meeting on September 15,1982. 
Clendenen Engineers accepted this invitation (see attached). 
At the meeting on September 15, 1982, each firm submitted 
its proposals. 
That submitted by Clendenen Engineers was for the 
MODULAR FILTER SYSTEM. 
At this time, Mr. Clendenen informed the 
Board that he was the President and principal shareholder in Sierra 
Tech Systems, the manufacturer of the modular filter system. 
CH2M 
Hill of Redding and GDA Engineering submitted plans which did not 
include the modular filter system. 
On the basis of the information 
submitted to the Board at the September 15, 1982, meeting, it decided 
to retain Clendenen Engineers to prepare a pre-design report for 
the Paradise Irrigation District Water Treatment Plant. 


CREVE. CLIFFORD. DIEPENBROCK S PARAS 
Barbara Milman 
April 7, 1983 
Page three 
that: 
Section 87.100 of the Political Reform Act provides 
IINo public official at any level of state 
or local government shall make, participate in 
making, or in any way attempt to use an official 
position to influence a governmental decision in 
which he knows or has reason to know he has a 
financial interest. 1I 
As you are aware, a willful violation of this provi-
sion is a misdemeanor, and any decision in violation of this 
section may be enjoined. 
(GC §91000 (b) ). 
In the common meaning of the word, neither Mr. Clendenen 
nor Clendenen Engineers is a "public official ll within the meaning 
of the Act. 
However, GC §82048 states that: 
'" [PJublic official' means every member, 
officer, and employee or consultant of a state 
or local government agency.1I 
It is clear under California law that neither Mr. 
Clendenen nor Clendenen Engineers is a consultant within the 
meaning of the Political Reform Act. 
California Administrative Code Section 18700 defines 
a Hconsultant ll as follows: 
HI [C)onsultant' shall include any natural 
person who provides, under contract, information, 
advice, recommendation, or counsel to a state or 
local government agency, provided, however, that 
'consultant' shall not include a person who' (a) 
conducts research and arrives at conclusions with 
respect to his or her rendetion of information, 
advice, recommendation, or counsel independent of 
the control and direction of the agency or of any 
agency official other than normal contract monitor-
ing; and (b) possesses no authority with respect to 
agency decision beyond the rendetion of information, 
advice, recommendation, or counsel. III 
In light of this definition, Clendenen Engineers is 
not a consultant within the meaning of the Political Reform Act 
for three reasons: 
1. 
Clendenen Engineers is a corporation --
a legal individual, but not a natural person as defined 
by the California Administrative Code; 


CREVE, CLIFFORD, DIEPENBROCK &3 PARAS 
Barbara Milman 
April 7, 1982 
Page four 
2. 
At the request of the PID, Clendenen 
Engineers presented its information, advice, recom-
mendation, and counsel, independent of the control 
and direction of the Agency. 
Therefore, it is not 
a consultant with the meaning of the statute; and, 
3. 
Clendenen possesses no authority with 
respect to any Agency decision beyond its presenta-
tion of its recommendations concerning the water 
treatment plant. 
Clendenen Engineers was requested by the PID to make 
a proposal for the construction of a water filtration plant. 
It 
did so, with Mr. Clendenen fully explaining his involvement in 
the proposed modular filter system manufactured by Sierra Tech 
Systems, Inc. 
This was a presentation made to the Board which 
was accepted. 
It was not a procedure whereby Mr. Clendenen or 
Clendenen Engineers was sought as a consultant for the prepara-
tion of an unspecified water treatment plant. 
No provision of the 
Political Reform Act supports a finding that Clendenen Engineers 
or Mr. Frank Clendenen is a consultant subject to a potential 
conflict of interest. 
Assuming that neither Clendenen Engineers nor Mr. Frank 
Clendenen is a consultant within the meaning of the statute, no 
conflict of interest exists. 
Under California Administrative Code 
Section 18702(c) a material financial effect and, therefore, a 
conflict of interest does not exist where modification, perpetua-
tion, or renewal of a contractual relationship or the opportunity 
to bid competitively is the result of the consultation. 
At the 
time Mr. Clendenen's proposal was accepted by the Irrigation 
District, it made its determination to employ the Modular Filtra-
tion System. 
Therefore, the retention of Clendenen Engineers for 
the completed design of the water treatment plant is only perpetua-
tion of the proposal which was accepted by the Paradise Irrigation 
District. 
The governmental decision which is crucial to the en-
forcement of the Political Reform Act was made prior to the reten-
tion of Clendenen Engineers by the District. 
Neither Mr. Clendenen 
nor his firm had any input in the making of a governmental decision. 
At present, Clendenen Engineers and the PID wish to 
enter into an agreement for the design of the filtration plant 
system 
The Distirct has already determined that it wished to 
implement the Modular Filtration System. 
Therefore, neither Mr. 
Clendenen nor his firm had any input or influence as to whether 
the system should be used -- the decision has already been made. 
Mr. Clendenen fully disclosed his involvement in the invention 
of the modular filter system at the time he made his original 
presentation to the Board. 
The incorporation of the system into 
the plans and spec 
ications called for by the proposed agreement 
does not create any conflict of interest. 
This decision was made 


CREVE. CLIFFORD. DIEPENBROCK &3 PARAS 
Barbara Milman 
April 7, 1983 
Page five 
long before Mr. Clendenen engaged in activity which could possibly 
be construed as "consulting" within the meaning of the Political 
Reform Act 
With respect to the modular filter system itself, 
Government Code Section 4380 provides that the contractor awarded 
the construction contract for the PID water treatment plant may 
submit a request for the substitution of the system. 
As this 
request for substitution would be evaluated and voted upon by the 
Irrigation District Board rather than Clendenen Engineers, no undue 
influence would exist in this respect. 
While we are confident in our analysis that no conflict 
exists within the meaning of the Political Reform Act, we are 
cognizant of the fact that the Commission requests substantiating 
data as to the interest of Mr. Clendenen in Sierra Tech Systems, 
Inc. 
As noted above, Mr. Clendenen is the President and sole 
shareholder of that firm. 
Sierra Tech Systems, Inc., was incor-
porated on June 30, 1980. 
Due to the youth of the corporation, 
a clear financial picture is not possible. 
While annual sales in 
fiscal 1981-1982 were approximately $396,000, these were signifi-
cantly offset by developing costs. 
The sale of the modular filter 
system to the PID would lead to a material income for the corpora-
tion. 
However, the system has been previously sold and tested, 
and its use by the Irrigation District would not lead to any sub-
stantial profits in excess of the basic sale amount. 
We trust that this correspondence sets forth all of the 
information necessary for the Fair Political Practices Commission 
to render its opinion. 
Your consideration of this matter is 
greatly appreciated. 
We will be more than happy to answer any 
further questions necessary for a resolution of this matter. 
ETC:lbc 


DAVID H. MINASIAN. 
PAUL R. MINASIAN. IN 
WILLIAM H. SPRUANCE. INC. 
WILLIAM H. BABER III. INC. 
JEFFREY A. MEITH 
M. ANTHONY SOARES 
MINASIAN, MINASIAN, MINASIAN, SPRUANCE, BABER & MEITH 
ATTORNEYS AT LAW 
COMPOSED OF PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS) 
1 BIRD STREET AT OAK STREET 
p. O. BOX 1679 
OROVILLE. CALIFORNIA 95965-1679 
TELEPHONE (916) 533·2885 
March 2, 1983 
JOHN CARLETON GRAY. U72.1018 
CARLETON GRAY. 180.!,'P44 
PAUL JACKSON MINASIAN. 1G33.1G81 
Board of Directors 
Paradise Irrigation District 
P. O. Box 128 
Paradise, California 95969 
Re: 
Clendenen Conflict of Interest 
Gentlemen: 
It is my understanding that in or about November 
of 1982, Mr. Frank Clendenen made a presentation to the 
Board of Directors of the Paradise Irrigation District, 
during which he offered his engineering services and proposed 
that the Board undertake the construction of a modular 
filtration system. 
The proposed modular filtr~J:.igJl.sy~tem, 
or parts or equipment: l.ncluded tfiereiif:-~rs-apparently 
patented, with the patent being held by an entity in which 
Mr. Clendenen is an officer or from which he receives income. 
I am informed that this financial interest was disclosed to 
the Board at the outset by Mr. Clendenen. 
The Board apparently accepted Mr. Clendenen's 
proposal and authorized him to prepare a Pre-Design Report 
describing, among other things, a 6MGD modular treatment 
plant. 
The Pre-Design Report has been completed and sub-
mitted to the Board. 
There is now being prepared an agreement 
between the District" and Mr. Clendenen under which the 
latter party will, among other things, perform the following 
work relating to the construction of the proposed modular 
treatment plant: 
1. 
Prepare final plans, specifications, and 
costs estimates. 
Assist in securing and 
analyzing bids and make recommendation for 
award. 
Assist in preparation of contract 
documents. 
2. 
Provide contract administration of project 
construction to observe compliance with 
plans, specifications, and all other contract 
documents. 
(See Proposed Agreement, Exhibit "B".) 


Board of Directors 
March 2, 1983 
Page Two 
The question has been asked: 
Does Mr. Clendenen, 
under the circumstances set forth above, have a conflict of 
\ 
interest. 
Although this question does not lend itself to an 
absolute and unequivocal answer, it is our opinion that Mr. 
Clendenen does not generally have a conflict of interest. 
However, because a reasonable argument for a contrary conclusion 
can be made and because there exists a substantial likelihood 
that any agreement with Mr. Clendenen would be challeged on 
this basis, we 
recQ].IllIlelld~,-th9:t~~r. 
Clen5:l~Il~n b~~_al~Jtedto 
both the prob~nd the procedures set forth in Section E 
-of tfie Paradise Irrl"gationuistrTc( Conflict of Interest 
~cmte-punua:rrt~6-wrrrch ne~~rn~ay request from the Fair Political 
~ract1ces C6mrnd~slon (the administrative agency charged with 
the enforcement and implementation of the Political Reform 
Act) an opinion as to whether a violation exists. 
(Attached 
is a copy of Section E.) 
Since 1974, the primary source of conflict of 
interest law has been the Political Reform Act, Government 
Code Section 81000, et seq. 
The general prohibition found 
in Section 87100 provides as follows: 
"No public official at any level of 
state or local government shall make, 
participate in making, or in any way 
attempt to use an official position to 
influence a governmental decision in 
which he knows or has reason to know he 
has a financial interest." 
(Emphasis 
added) 
A knowing and willful violation of this provision is a 
misdemeanor, and the tainted governmental decision may be 
enjoined. 
(Government Code Sections 91000, 91003 (b).) 
The term public official is defined in Section 
82048 as follows: 
"'Public official' means every member, 
officer and employee or consultant of a 
state or local government agency." 
(Emphasis added) 
The term consultant is defined in Section 18700 of 
Title 2 of the California Administrative Code as follows: 


Board of Directors 
March 2, 1983 
Page Three 
"'Consultant' shall include any natural 
person who provides, under contract, 
information, advice, recommendation or 
counsel to a state or local government 
agency, provided, however, that 'consultant' 
shall not include a person who: 
(A) 
Conducts research and arrives at conclusions 
with respect to his or her rendition of 
information, advice, recommendation or 
counsel independent of the control and 
direction of the agency or of any agency 
official, other than normal contract 
monitoring; and (B) Possesses no authority 
with respect to any agency decision 
beyond the rendition of information, 
advice, recommendation or counsel." 
Thus. Mr. Clendenen is a consultant \olithin the 
meaning or the Poll.tiCa1-"RefO~rm -A~~~c!Ild'~rfs'Implementi.ng 
'-regulat1on~e prOViae-s"-Trifo riilat ion , advice, recommendation 
, or counsel to-the Board, under contract, unless his research 
l
and opinions are conducted and formed independently and he 
possess no authority with respect to District decisions 
\ 
beyond the provision of information, advise, recommendation, 
or counsel. 
If Mr. Clendenen's research and opinions are 
not independent or if he has additional decision making 
authority, he would be, to that extent, a consultant. 
Even if Mr. Clendenen is, to some extent, a con-
sultant, it does not necessarily follow that he possesses an 
unlawful conflict of interest. 
Rather, it must then be 
determined whether a governmental decision in which he is 
involved would have a material financial effect on his 
financial interest .. Section l8702(c) of Title 2 of the 
-California Administrative Code defines a "material financial 
effect" as it applies to a consultant: 
" 
. the making or participation in 
the making of a governmental decision by 
a contract consultant or by a person 
retained to provide information, advice, 
recommendation or counsel has no material 
financial effect on a business entity or 
source of income in which such consultant 
or· person retained is an officer, employee, 
sole proprietor or partner, if the only 
financial effects of the decision are 
modification, perpetuation or renewal of 
the contractual or retainer agreement 


Board of Directors 
March 2, 1983 
Page Four 
and/or the opportunity to bid competitively 
on a project or contract." 
Thus, a material financial effect does not exist 
when only modification, perpetuation or renewal of a contractual 
arrangement or the opportunity to bid competively on a 
project result. 
However, to the extent that a govermental 
decision, made or participated in by Mr. Clendenen affects 
not only provisions of his engineering services, but also 
his financial interest in the entity holding a patent on 
particular filtration system parts o~ equipment, the potential 
for a conflict of interest exists. 
The principles reflected in the above quoted 
statutes and regulations must, then, be applied to the facts 
and circumstances of Mr. Clendenen's situation. 
Mr. Clendenen's 
activities, past and future, can and should be analyzed in 
three stages. 
The first stage concerns the initial presenta-
tion of his proposal to the Board. 
Mr. Clendenen was not at 
that stage pr~viding information and advice under contract. 
Rather, he was simply a vendor of engineering services and a 
particular filtration system. 
As 
such~ he was not a consultant 
or public official and was not subject to the Act. 
(CF. 3 
FPPC Opinions 71, p.3.) 
After the first stage, Mr. Clendenen was authorized 
by the Board to prepare a Pre-Design Report describing the 
proposed modular treatment plant. 
At this pre-design stage, 
Mr. Clendenen was providing information and advice to the 
Board. 
However, since his research and opinions were conducted 
and formed independently and since his participation in 
government decisions did not amount to more than the providing 
of information and advice, once again, he was not a consultant 
or public official and was not subject to the Act. 
The third stage of activity will involve the 
implementation of the modular treatment plant proposal. 
Inasmuch as Mr. Clendenen may, in addition to providing 
information and advice, make or participate in decisions 
during this stage, the potential for conflict exists. 
Although each governmental decision would need be evaluated 
in the light of Mr. Clendenen's activity or role and his 
financial interest, we do not believe the mere incorporation 
of th~ patented parts and equipment into the plans and 
specifications would violate the conflict of.interest 
provlslons. 
As a practical matter, this decision was made 
by the Board during the first stage of Mr. Clendenen's 
activity, before he became a "consultant". 


Board of Directors 
March 2, 1983 
Page Five 
As we have indicated, there is the potential for 
conflict during the third stage of activity. 
For example, 
Government Code Section 4380 provides: 
"No agency of the state nor any political 
subdivision, municipal corporation, or 
district, nor any public officer or 
person charged with the letting of 
contracts for the construction, alteration, 
or repair of public works shall draft or 
cause to be drafted specifications for 
bids, in connection with the construction, 
alteration, or repair of public works, 
(1) in such a manner as to limit the 
bidding, direet1y or indirectly, to any 
one specific concern, or (2) except in 
those instances where the product is 
designated to match others in use on a 
particular public improvement either 
completed or in the course of completion, 
calling for a designated material, 
product, thing, or service by specific 
brand or trade name unless the specification 
lists at least two brands or trade names 
of comparable quality or utility and is 
followed by the words 'or equal' so that 
bidders may furnish any equal material, 
product, thing, or service. 
In applying 
this section, the specifying agency 
shall, if aware of an equal product 
manufactured in California, name such 
\ 
product in the specification. 
In those 
cases involving a unique or novel product 
application required to be used in the 
public interest, or where only one brand 
or trade name is known to the specifying 
agency, it may list only one. 
Specifications 
shall provide a period of time of at 
least 35 days after award of the contract 
for submission of data substantiating a 
request for a substitution of 'an equal' 
item. " 
We have been advised, and are assuming, that the 
patented parts or equipment are unique or novel items, and, 
therefore, lie within the exception to the general prohibition 
of Section 4380. 
Nonetheless, the contractor awarded the 


Board of Directors 
March 2, 1983 
Page Six 
construction contract may, by statute, submit a request for 
substitution and data supporting such request. 
In the light 
of Mr. Clendenen's interest in the entity supplying the 
\ 
parts or equipment incorporated into the plans and specifi-
cations, we would recommend that he not be involved in any 
decision concerning a request for substitution. 
Finally, another, and arguably more restrictive, 
conflict of interest law should be mentioned. 
Government 
Code Section 1090 provides: 
"Members of the Legislature, state, 
county, district, judicial district, and 
city officers or employees shall not be 
financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, 
or by any body or board of which they 
are members. 
Nor shall state, county, 
district, judicial district, and city 
officers or employees be purchasers at 
any sale or vendors at any purchase made 
by them in their official capacity. 
As used in the Article 'district' means 
any agency of the state formed pursuant 
to general law or special act, for the 
local performance of governmental or 
proprietary functions within limited 
boundaries." 
The courts of this State have held that a person 
merely in an advisory position to a public agency is affected 
by this conflict of interest rule. 
(Schaefer v. Berinstein 
(1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278, 291) 
They have also held that 
the word "made" is not used in the statute in its narrower 
and technical contract sense but is used in its broad sense 
to encompass such embodiment in the making of a contract as 
preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reason-
ing, planning, drawing of plans and specifications and 
solicitations for bids. 
Millbrae Assn. for Residental 
Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968y-262 Cal.App.2d 222. 
A 
contract or transaction entered into in violation of Section 
1090 is void. 
(Government Code Section 1092) 
The prohibition 
found in Section 1090 has apparently survived the enactment 
of the Political Reform Act. 
It has been stated informally 
by the Attorney General's Office that such continues to 


Board of Directors 
Harch 2, 1983 
Page Seven 
apply in contractual situations, while all other governmental 
decisions would be subject to the Political Reform Act. 
However, the Political Reform Act specifically provides that 
it supersedes other conflicting provisions. 
It is our 
opinion that in the light of the detailed handling of 
consultant transactions under the Political Reform Act and 
the implementing regulations, the provisions of Section 1090 
are superseded and compliance with the Political Reform Act 
will suffice. 
Notwithstanding our oplnlon that a conflict does 
not exist, and in the light of the potential sanctions, we 
direct your attention to Section E of the Paradise Irrigation 
District Conflict of Interest Code, titled "Opinions of the 
Commission and Counsel", which provides a procedure whereby 
any official that is unsure of his duty, right, or privilege 
of participation, may request from the Fair Political Practices 
Commission an opinion regarding a political conflict of 
interest. 
The Section also provides that the attorney for 
the District may file with such request its proposed opinion. 
We recommend that Mr. Clendenen be apprised of the potential 
problem and his option to request an FPPC opinion clarifying 
the matter under the Code. 
If you have any further questions or if you other-
wise wish to discuss this matter, please do not hesitate to 
contact us.

sections.conclusion: Because Mr. Clendenen is not a consultant, he is not subject to the conflict of interests provisions of the Act. However, in the future, if the District hires an engineer to work on other than a particular project, or provides an engineer with more authority than Mr. Clendenen possesses in the present situation, the District should contact us for additional advice as to whether these engineers are consultants within the meaning of the Act.
sections.analysis:
Government Code Section 871001/ prohibits a local govern-
ment official from making, or participating in the making of, a 
governmental decision in whicn he knows or has reason to know he 
has a financial interest. 
An official has a financial interest 
in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is 
reasonably foreseeable that tne decision will have a material 
financial effect on: 
!/ Hereinafter Edward T. Clifford 
Terry Appling 
May 10, 1983  
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment wortn more 
than $1,000.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Frank Clendenen, president and 100% shareholder of Sierra Tech Systems Inc., has a conflict of interest serving as general engineer for Paradise Irrigation District's water filtration system project when Sierra Tech manufactures the proposed filtration system.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mr. Clendenen does not have a conflict of interest because he is not a consultant under the Political Reform Act—he was hired to construct a specific project according to contract specifications, not to participate in governmental decisions. However, future engagements with different scope or authority levels should be reviewed separately.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: Document contains both incoming request letter (April 7, 1983) from Greve, Clifford law firm and FPPC response letter (May 10, 1983). Extracted information from FPPC response portion. Clear Question and Conclusion sections present. Document discusses consultant exemption under Government Code Section 87100 and California Administrative Code Section 18700.

CONCLUSION: Because Mr. Clendenen is not a consultant, he is not subject to the conflict of interests provisions of the Act. However, in the future, if the District hires an engineer to work on other than a particular project, or provides an engineer with more authority than Mr. Clendenen possesses in the present situation, the District should contact us for additional advice as to whether these engineers are consultants within the meaning of the Act.
embedding.first_500_words: P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance (916) 322.5662 Edward T. Clifford Ter ry Appl ing Administration 322·5660 Greve, Clifford, DiepenbrQck and Paras 1000 G Street, Suite 400 . Sacramento~ CA 95814-a885 Executive/legol 322·5901 May 10, 1983 Enforcement 322·6441 Statements of Economic I nteres! 322-6444 Re: Our Advice No. A-83-103 Dear Mr. Clifford and Ms. Appling: Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of the Paradise Irrigation District (hereinafter "District"). The facts, as presented to me, are that the Distrlct is going to improve its water filtration system. General plans were sub- mitted by, among otners, Frank Clendenen, the president and principal shareholder of Clendenen Engineers. The system he proposed is a "Modular Filtration System," which is manufactured by Sierra Tech Systems, Inc. Mr. Clendenen is the president and 100% shareholder of tnis company. The District approved Mr. Clendenen's proposal and he has been nired to act as tne general engineer for the construction of the system. Your question is whetner Mr. Clendenen will nave a conflict of interest in completing the contract because of his financial interest in Sierra Tech Systems, Inc. DISCUSSION Government Code Section 871001/ prohibits a local govern- ment official from making, or participating in the making of, a governmental decision in whicn he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that tne decision will have a material financial effect on: !/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Edward T. Clifford Terry Appling May 10, 1983 Page 2 (a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment wortn more than $1,000. * * * (c) Any source of income ... aggregating $250 or more in value provided to the pUblic official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. ( d) official Any business entity in which the public is ... [an] officer .... For purposes of the Political Reform Act, the term "public official" includes consultants of local government agencies. (2 Ca 1. Adm. Code Sect~on 18700 (a) .) A "consul tant" is a natural person who provides, under contract, information, advice, recommendation or counsel to a local government agency. (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (a) (2) . ) This term does not include a person who: (A) Conducts researCh and arrives at conclusions witn respect to his or her rendition of information, advice, recommendation or counsel independent of the control and direction of the agency or any agency official, other than normal contract monitoring; and (B) Possesses no authority with respect to any agency decision beyond the rendition of information, advice, recommendation or counsel. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a) (2) (A) and (B). When an engineer is hired under a·contract to perform engineering services for a specific project, he ordinarily comes within
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter A-83-103 addresses whether Frank Clendenen has a conflict of interest when hired as general engineer for Paradise Irrigation District's water filtration system while holding financial interest in Sierra Tech Systems, the manufacturer of the proposed filtration system. The FPPC concludes no conflict exists because Clendenen is performing professional engineering services on a specific project rather than serving as a consultant making governmental decisions.
